ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for stir-fried long bean with sambal; mapped to the closest parent class 'stir-fried vegetable dish' (FOODON_03311737). Exact entry does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (legume vegetables, cooked) used as the closest defensible anchor. No specific FoodEx2 entry exists for sambal-stir-fried long bean.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept identified for this specific dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard side dish serving of stir-fried long bean with sambal is approximately 250 g, typically presented as a shared vegetable side at a hawker centre or casual dining setting in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Cowpeas (Blackeyes), Immature Seeds, Cooked, Boiled, Drained, Without Salt (FDC ID 170390) and Long Beans, Cooked (SR Legacy); adapted for sambal stir-fry preparation
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for stir-fried long bean with sambal exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore Foodo as a composite dish. Nutrient values from the input record (grabfood provenance, confidence 0.7) have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked yard-long bean / cowpea (FDC ID 170390) and adjusted upward for fat and sodium to reflect stir-frying in cooking oil and the addition of sambal paste, belacan, and fish sauce. Energy 112 kcal, protein 2.4 g, fat 8.0 g, carbohydrate 7.2 g per 100 g are consistent with expected values for a stir-fried legume vegetable dish with sambal condiments. Potassium estimated at 240 mg/100 g based on USDA SR Legacy long bean ingredient data. HPB Singapore Foodo was also consulted as a secondary cross-check for regional plausibility.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2008;88(5):1553–1556. Analogous food: cooked yard-long bean / green bean, GI ~28.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried long bean with sambal. GI estimated at 28 (low) based on the closest analogous food: cooked yard-long bean / green bean, GI approximately 28–32, as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Am J Clin Nutr 88:1553–1556). Sambal paste and cooking oil do not materially raise GI. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (28 × 18.0) / 100 = 5.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100 g from belacan, fish sauce, and sambal paste; partially offset by estimated potassium ~240 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced belacan and fish sauce quantities, as sodium at 300 mg/100 g (750 mg per 250 g serving) approaches 33% of the 2,300 mg daily limit in a single side dish; the estimated potassium content (~600 mg per serving) provides partial cardiovascular offset but does not fully neutralise the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~28), modest carbohydrate 7.2 g/100 g, and dietary fibre 2.0 g/100 g moderating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried long bean with sambal is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycemic index (~28), modest carbohydrate content (18 g per 250 g serving), and 5 g dietary fibre per serving that slows glucose absorption; however, sambal paste may contain added sugar and should be requested in reduced quantity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100 g and total fat 8.0 g/100 g; saturated fat is low at 1.4 g/100 g and trans fat is absent
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish presents a moderate cardiovascular concern primarily due to sodium (300 mg/100 g) from sambal condiments; saturated fat is low at 1.4 g/100 g and trans fat is absent, which is favourable, but individuals with CVD should request reduced sambal and belacan to keep the per-serving sodium below 500 mg.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory dietary fibre and vitamin C from long beans; low saturated fat; nightshade chilli present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Long beans provide vitamin C (10 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre (2.0 g/100 g) with anti-inflammatory properties; saturated fat is low at 1.4 g/100 g and trans fat is absent, but RA-sensitive individuals should monitor their response to chilli (a nightshade) as solanine may aggravate symptoms in susceptible persons.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried Long Bean with Sambal (叁巴炒长豆; Kacang Panjang Goreng Sambal) is a popular Singaporean and Southeast Asian vegetable side dish in which yard-long beans (Vigna unguiculata subsp. sesquipedalis) are wok-stir-fried at high heat with a fragrant sambal paste made from dried chillies, belacan (fermented shrimp paste), shallots, garlic, and dried shrimp, often finished with fish sauce and a squeeze of lime. The dish delivers a bold, spicy, savoury, and slightly smoky flavour profile with a tender-crisp texture. It is commonly served as a shared side dish at hawker centres, coffee shops, and casual restaurants across Singapore and Malaysia. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 112 kcal, 2.4 g protein, 8.0 g fat (1.4 g saturated, 0 g trans), 7.2 g carbohydrate, 3.2 g sugar, 2.0 g dietary fibre, 300 mg sodium, 32 mg calcium, 0.6 mg iron, and 10 mg vitamin C. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 28 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. The primary clinical concern is sodium from belacan and fish sauce; individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease should request reduced condiment quantities. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to belacan and dried shrimp, and contains shellfish and fish allergens.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from input record with grabfood provenance at confidence 0.7, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked yard-long bean (FDC ID 170390) with adjustments for stir-frying in oil and sambal condiments. No exact composite dish entry exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore Foodo for this specific preparation; values are consistent with the expected range for this dish category. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 for cooked yard-long bean. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this stir-fried sambal vegetable dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore Foodo as of 2024. 2. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation not reported in available approved sources. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient detail for this specific dish preparation not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore Foodo as of 2024. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/absent in vegetable oil-based stir-fry preparations; USDA FoodData Central reports 0 g trans fat for cooked yard-long bean and standard cooking oils. 5. Potassium estimated at 240 mg/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy long bean and shallot ingredient data; not directly measured for this composite dish. 6. GI of 28 is an analogous estimate based on cooked yard-long bean from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Am J Clin Nutr 88:1553–1556); no direct GI measurement for this specific sambal preparation exists. 7. The dish is labelled 'Thai-style' in the input description and the input cultural field lists primary_cuisine as Thai; however, the dish is sold in Singapore, uses belacan (a Malaysian/Singaporean ingredient), and is classified here as Singaporean/Southeast Asian. Thai-script alias has been included to reflect the Thai-style preparation claim. Multilingual coverage includes English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as required for Singaporean dishes, plus Thai as noted. 8. Halal status is marked false because belacan and dried shrimp sourcing cannot be confirmed as halal-certified without vendor verification. 9. Nutrient source is USDA FoodData Central (approved secondary global source) used as the primary evidence base for this dish, as no exact composite dish entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo. HPB Singapore Foodo was consulted as a secondary cross-check for regional plausibility.
